title: Building AI Features in Web Apps

questions.question: Why should LLM API calls always be made from the server (API route) rather than directly from client-side browser code?
options: API keys embedded in client-side code can be extracted by anyone using browser DevTools, leading to unauthorized usage and unexpected costs
LLM APIs only accept requests from server IP addresses and block all browser-origin requests
Browser JavaScript is too slow to parse LLM responses efficiently
Client-side API calls always fail due to CORS restrictions that cannot be configured
questions.explanation: The primary reason is security. Any API key included in client-side JavaScript (even via environment variables like NEXT_PUBLIC_*) gets bundled into the code that's sent to the browser. Anyone can open DevTools, find the key, and use it to make unlimited API calls at your expense. Server-side routes keep the key secret and also let you add rate limiting, input validation, and logging.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of streaming LLM responses to the browser using Server-Sent Events (SSE) instead of waiting for the complete response?
options: Streaming reduces the total number of tokens generated by the LLM
Streaming allows users to see the first words within ~1 second instead of waiting 5-15 seconds for the full response, creating a much better perceived performance
Streaming eliminates the need for error handling since partial responses are always usable
SSE connections use less bandwidth than standard HTTP requests
questions.explanation: Streaming doesn't reduce the total generation time or token count -- it improves perceived performance. Instead of staring at a spinner for 10 seconds, users see the first word almost immediately and can start reading while the rest generates. The total time is the same, but the experience feels dramatically faster.

questions.question: When implementing retry logic for failed LLM API calls, why is 'jitter' (adding a random component to the delay) important?
options: Jitter makes the retry delays unpredictable so the LLM provider cannot detect automated retries
Jitter reduces the total number of retry attempts needed
Without jitter, many clients that hit a rate limit simultaneously will all retry at the same time, causing a 'thundering herd' that overwhelms the API again
Jitter is required by the HTTP specification for 429 responses
questions.explanation: Without jitter, if 100 clients are all rate-limited at the same moment, they'll all retry after exactly 1 second, then 2 seconds, then 4 seconds -- hitting the API with the same burst each time. Adding a random delay (jitter) spreads the retries across the time window, reducing the chance of another rate limit and letting the API recover.

questions.question: In the circuit breaker pattern for AI API calls, what happens when the circuit is in the 'open' state?
options: All requests are sent to the API normally but with a longer timeout
Requests are immediately rejected without calling the API, preventing further load on a failing service
Requests are queued in memory and sent in bulk when the circuit closes
The application switches to a backup LLM provider automatically
questions.explanation: When the circuit is 'open', it means the service has failed repeatedly (e.g., 5 consecutive failures). The circuit breaker immediately rejects new requests without making the API call, returning an error to the user. After a timeout period (e.g., 30 seconds), the circuit transitions to 'half-open' and allows a few test requests through. If those succeed, the circuit closes and normal operation resumes.

questions.question: A user sends a message in your AI chat app and sees: 'Error: AxiosError: Request failed with status code 429 - rate_limit_exceeded - You have exceeded your current usage tier limit.' What is wrong with this error message?
options: The error message is too short and should include the full API response body
The message exposes internal implementation details (library name, raw API error). Users should see a friendly message like 'You're sending messages too quickly. Wait a moment and try again.'
The 429 status code is incorrect; rate limits should return a 503 status
Nothing is wrong -- users should see the exact error from the API so they can troubleshoot
questions.explanation: Raw API errors should never reach the user. This message leaks internal details: the HTTP client library (Axios), the status code, and the provider's error format. Users don't know what a 429 is or what 'usage tier' means. Instead, map errors to clear, actionable messages: 'You're sending messages too quickly. Wait a moment and try again.' This is more helpful and doesn't expose your implementation.

questions.question: For streaming LLM responses to a browser, why is Server-Sent Events (SSE) preferred over WebSockets?
options: SSE supports higher data throughput than WebSockets
SSE is simpler and sufficient because LLM streaming is server-to-client only -- no bidirectional communication is needed
WebSockets are not supported in modern browsers
SSE encrypts data automatically while WebSockets do not
questions.explanation: LLM streaming is inherently one-directional: the server generates tokens and sends them to the client. SSE is purpose-built for this pattern -- it's simpler than WebSockets (just HTTP), requires no upgrade handshake, works with standard infrastructure (load balancers, proxies, CDNs), and includes automatic reconnection. WebSockets add unnecessary complexity for a use case that doesn't need bidirectional communication.

questions.question: What is the serverless function timeout for streaming functions on Vercel's free tier?
options: 10 seconds, the same as standard serverless functions
30 seconds, with 60 seconds available on paid plans
60 seconds -- streaming functions get a longer timeout even on the free tier
There is no timeout for streaming functions on any tier
questions.explanation: Vercel's free tier allows 10 seconds for standard serverless functions, but streaming functions get 60 seconds even on the free tier. This is critical for AI features because LLM responses can take 10-30 seconds to fully generate. You can set this with `export const maxDuration = 60;` in your route file.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using the Vercel AI SDK (`ai` package) for building AI chat features in Next.js?
options: It provides access to AI models that are not available through standard APIs
It reduces the cost of LLM API calls by compressing tokens
It handles streaming, state management, and error handling for AI chat in React/Next.js, replacing significant custom code
It is required by Vercel for deploying any application that uses AI features
questions.explanation: The Vercel AI SDK abstracts away the complexity of streaming AI responses, managing chat state, and handling errors. On the server, `streamText()` handles the LLM call and SSE formatting. On the client, `useChat()` manages messages, input state, and streaming -- replacing hundreds of lines of custom hook code. Understanding the underlying mechanics is important (which is why we teach them), but for production apps, the SDK saves significant development time.
